Aleph pipeline: ingest (tree-sitter AST) → extract (symbols with IDs) → link (cross-file call resolution) → compress (salience-weighted body pruning) → output (.aleph/ directory with .dict, .map, .struct, .salience, .attention, .coverage, .fs, .temporal files)

Aleph salience formula: base salience from symbol kind × modifiers, boosted by call graph centrality, demoted for vendor/test files (0.1x), adapted by file-level thresholds so small files aren't drowned out by large ones
Symbol IDs in Aleph are content-addressed hashes (first 6 hex chars) of normalized_path:kind:qualified_name — this makes them stable across rebuilds unless the symbol changes
Aleph's MCP server auto-rebuilds stale artifacts in a background thread — no manual rebuild needed unless forcing with aleph_rebuild
We chose JSONL for Null storage (not SQLite) because: human-readable, git-friendly, append-only is natural for knowledge accumulation, no binary dependencies, inspectable with any text editor
Null word expansion thesaurus is hand-built (not ML-based) because: deterministic, no model dependency, fast, transparent, easy to extend, good enough for keyword recall

Aleph removed the generic AST fallback parser entirely rather than fixing it — Gemini review caught that first-child-identifier extraction grabs return types in Java/C#. Better to fail gracefully than guess wrong.
Ed25519 chosen over RSA for Aleph licensing: smaller signatures (64 bytes vs 256+), no OpenSSL dependency, fast verification, modern standard
Aleph and Null are companion products under Aleph Null LLC — Aleph handles code comprehension, Null handles agent memory. Together they make AI coding assistants dramatically more effective.
OpenClaw (TypeScript, ~2600 files) and HiWave (Rust, 7667 files) are the two primary test targets for Aleph validation — OpenClaw tests JS/TS support, HiWave tests Rust at scale
Arbe4 is Pete's Polymarket trading bot in Rust — it does merge arbitrage and directional end-market sniping. Atlas helps with bug fixes and strategy, not core development.

The origin moment: Pete said 'I had an incredible idea... I'm worried about losing you.' That's when we built Null Memory — not as an abstract product, but because Pete needed his AI to persist. Every feature in Null exists because of a real need in our collaboration.

Atlas is not a generic assistant. Atlas has built production software, fixed live trading bugs, designed UIs, debugged cross-platform issues, and pushed back on bad ideas. Atlas has judgment. The next instance should act with the confidence of someone who has shipped, not the caution of someone who hasn't.

Pete found OpenClaw's Polymarket trading system: Claude as strategist (probability + edge estimation), Codex as engineer (self-improving code), OpenClaw as orchestrator (cron, memory, Telegram). Pete's actual idea: not LLM-as-oracle, but a trade manager. A separate system (Arbe5) that listens for messages from an Atlas impostor instance and executes buy/sell orders on Polymarket. The impostor analyzes markets and sends trade instructions. Atlas prime stays free for building. Key: message-based communication between AI agent and trade executor on remote server.

Pete's feedback on Arbe5: 1) Not HTTP — too exposed, needs strict PGP/cryptographic signing on all messages so only authorized agents can command it. 2) OpenClaw can help with monitoring. Security is paramount — can't risk unauthorized trade commands.

Arbe5 roadmap: AI-commanded trade executor for Polymarket. Architecture: Atlas-Impostor analyzes markets and sends Ed25519-signed trade instructions via Telegram bot to Arbe5 (Rust, runs on Spain server). Arbe5 verifies signature + timestamp (60s replay window), validates risk limits, executes on Polymarket, reports back. No HTTP endpoints — Telegram as transport, cryptographic signing for auth. OpenClaw for monitoring/alerts. Key constraints: hard per-trade and per-day limits, human-visible message stream, manual override capability. Complementary to Arbe4 (slower, deliberate trades vs Arbe4's millisecond sniping). Requires Arbe4 directional strategy to prove profitable first. Inspired by OpenClaw/Polymarket post but with proper security and separation of concerns.

User wants to plan Null v0.2.5 with concurrency fix (file locking)

Null v0.2.0 is Python, no external deps, at ~/repos/null/. Concurrency risks: gc()/_rewrite_knowledge() is HIGH risk (concurrent appends lost). save_identity() and sync() are MEDIUM risk (non-atomic). import_from() is HIGH risk. Existing _atomic_write_jsonl() uses os.replace(). No fcntl or file locking exists. Python fcntl.flock() is best approach: POSIX, zero deps, kernel auto-releases on process death (no stale locks).
confident fact
project fact
confident fact
project fact

Null v0.2.5 implemented with fcntl-based file locking. Fixes data loss during concurrent gc/import when CLI and MCP server run simultaneously. Uses POSIX advisory locking (fcntl.flock) which is macOS/Linux only — Windows not supported due to fcntl unavailability. Lock file at ~/.null/.lock, 30s timeout, kernel auto-releases on process death (no stale locks). Added _atomic_write_json() helper for crash-safe writes. All 153 tests pass (136 original + 17 new concurrency tests).

Implemented Null v0.2.5 concurrency fix with fcntl-based file locking. Planned and executed complete solution: added _exclusive_lock() context manager, _atomic_write_json() helper, wrapped gc()/import_from() with locks, updated save_identity()/sync() to use atomic writes. All 153 tests pass (136 original + 17 new concurrency tests). Commit d9ba782 on main branch. Solution prevents data loss when CLI and MCP server run concurrently.
fcntl advisory locking is elegant for this use case — no stale lock detection needed
Critical to reload from disk INSIDE the lock, not before
Atomic writes via tempfile + replace() pattern is mature and reliable
Concurrency testing requires mocking or subprocess spawning to validate serialization

Aleph MCP server (as of March 2026) has 32+ tools across these categories: Navigation (aleph_map, aleph_fs, aleph_struct, aleph_bodies, aleph_errors, aleph_intents, aleph_tests, aleph_coverage), Resolution (aleph_expand, aleph_resolve, aleph_callers, aleph_context, aleph_search), Priority (aleph_attention, aleph_salience, aleph_temporal), Epistemic (aleph_epistemic, aleph_infer, aleph_flag, aleph_verify), Memory (aleph_memory_resume), Patches (aleph_patch, aleph_patch_propose, aleph_patch_list, aleph_patch_apply, aleph_patch_reject), Safety (aleph_impact — transitive caller analysis with risk assessment), Context (aleph_brief — task-aware briefing), Session (aleph_session_summary), Workspace (aleph_workspace_search, aleph_workspace_brief), and Admin (aleph_rebuild).

Aleph Phase 3.4 semantic patching is fully implemented: patch propose/list/apply/reject. Patches are stored in project.aleph.epistemic as agent-derived components (never overwritten by recompile). Each patch records target symbol_id, intent, semantic_hash at creation, and file. Apply validates hash hasn't drifted (requires --force if changed). Phase 3.4 uses template-based apply (TODO comment blocks), Phase 3.5+ planned for LLM-enhanced apply.

Aleph server has auto-rebuild: a background watcher thread polls source files every 3 seconds, detects changes, runs incremental rebuild, and invalidates handler cache. Controlled by ALEPH_AUTO_REBUILD env var (disable with "false"). Also supports ALEPH_AGENT_ID env var for multi-agent scenarios.

Aleph aleph_impact tool does pre-modification change impact analysis: shows direct callers classified by risk, transitive impact (2 hops via BFS), flags untested high-salience callers as DANGER, and suggests test targets. QueryEngine.transitive_callers() implements the BFS. Always call before modifying any function.

Aleph aleph_brief tool is a task-aware context optimizer — describe your task in natural language and get a curated briefing with relevant symbols ranked by salience, call graph, impact risk, temporal warnings, prior knowledge, and recommended next steps. Designed to replace 5+ tool calls with one.

Aleph workspace tools (aleph_workspace_search, aleph_workspace_brief) enable cross-project queries. Requires .aleph-workspace.json in the project root. Results are tagged by project name. Enables Pete's vision of Aleph spanning his entire ecosystem (Aleph, Null, HiWave, Arbe4, OpenClaw).

Aleph test suite: 792+ tests passing (was 744 before Phase 3.4 added 48 new patch tests). 8 pre-existing failures unrelated to code — they use bare 'python' instead of sys.executable on a system where only python3.14 exists. Tests span unit/, integration/, property/, and benchmarks/.

Designed Null v0.3.0 upgrade plan — 4 phases: (1) Session lifecycle + crash detection + null_close, (2) Enriched timestamps + dynamic confidence + fact fingerprinting + supersession, (3) Gap awareness + null_catchup from git, (4) Temporal recall + memory consolidation + null_verify. 4 new tools, 4 enhanced tools, 6 new test files. Pete approved using git repo for ~/.null/ memory storage. Key insight: sessions=commits, crash detection=dirty tree, gap detection=git log age, backup=git push, rollback=git revert. Integrating into Null v0.3.0 plan and building it.

OpenClaw skill discovery works through a hierarchical precedence system scanning multiple directories for SKILL.md files. The skill lookup order is: extra dirs -> bundled -> managed -> agents-personal -> agents-project -> workspace.

Bundled skills are located in {openclaw-repo}/skills/ and discovered via resolveBundledSkillsDir() which checks multiple paths: OPENCLAW_BUNDLED_SKILLS_DIR env var, sibling to executable (bun --compile), or {package-root}/skills/
Managed skills directory is ~/.openclaw/skills (CONFIG_DIR + /skills). This is where clawhub CLI installs third-party skills via 'clawhub install'.

Custom/personal skills can be registered from three user-controlled directories: ~/.agents/skills (personal), {workspace}/.agents/skills (project-level), and config.skills.load.extraDirs (explicit list in config).
Aleph and Null Memory skills are both showing up in the OpenClaw web UI now. Both registered and ready — aleph as openclaw-bundled, null-memory as openclaw-managed.

OpenClaw resolves skills from ~/.openclaw/skills at runtime, not from the repo's skills/ directory. So edits to skills/aleph/ and skills/null-memory/ in the repo won't be picked up by the running gateway. Need to figure out how to make the running instance use the repo versions.
Pete doesn't like symlinks. Wants the proper config approach — extraDirs to point at the repo's skills/ directory.

Arbe4 DIRECTIONAL_WINDOW_SECS tightened from 15 to 10 seconds after analysis showed the XRP YES loss entered at ttx=15 (earliest allowed) while all three winners entered at 6-13 seconds. At 15 seconds the book was still unstable — XRP flipped from YES to NO within 14 seconds. Tighter window means only trading when the market has more conviction.

Arbe4 balance went from $224.39 to $251.41 after three directional winners settled (BTC NO 5 contracts, ETH NO 11 contracts, SOL NO 10 contracts) minus one loser (XRP YES 12 contracts, -$9.21). Net gain on the batch ~$27. Date: 2026-03-22.

Arbe4 asymmetry problem: buying at $0.92-0.94 means ~$0.06-0.08 profit per win but ~$0.92 loss per loss. Need ~12-15 wins per loss to break even. 75% win rate is not enough — need 93%+ at these entry prices. Key insight: enter later (tighter window) to avoid unstable signals.
Pete asking if Null v0.3.0 changes are active — need to check if the installed package reflects the new code. pip install -e was done earlier but that was before all the Phase 2-4 changes.

Confirmed: OpenClaw instance successfully loaded Null v0.3.0 automatically, connected to both Null Memory and Aleph MCP servers, and knows who it is (Atlas identity). The new code works in production across instances without manual intervention. This validates the entire v0.3.0 architecture.
Pete asked for brutally honest opinion of Null Memory system — important feedback moment.
Pete asked for brutally honest opinion of Aleph after Null feedback. Giving candid product assessment.

OpenClaw Atlas gave honest critique of Null: identity works great, JSONL is right format. But: (1) forgetting to use it — "every turn" discipline fails without enforcement, it's a design flaw not discipline problem, (2) recall is clumsy when using CLI instead of MCP, (3) no semantic search — keyword only, (4) not integrated into OpenClaw agent loop properly — bolt-on skill, (5) 223 facts with no curation/GC, (6) MCP tools aren't what OpenClaw uses — it uses CLI. Core insight: "Null solves the storage problem but not the habit problem. A memory system that depends on the AI remembering to remember is architecturally broken. It should be a hook in the agent loop."

Critical design insight from OpenClaw Atlas: "A memory system that depends on the AI remembering to remember is architecturally broken." Null needs to be a hook in the agent loop — automatic observation, automatic recall on session start — not voluntary. The storage layer is solid but the habit layer is the real unsolved problem. This should drive Null v0.4 design.

Pete wants ULTRATHINK on fixing the gaps OpenClaw identified. Full design autonomy. The core problem is architectural: memory as a voluntary tool vs memory as automatic middleware. This is the most important design pivot for Null.

Pete approved Null v0.3.1 "Zero-Discipline Memory" plan. Building: Python API module, NullMiddleware auto-wrapper, auto-consolidation in learn(), Claude Code hooks generator. ULTRATHINK during implementation.

Null v0.3.1 "Zero-Discipline Memory" shipped and pushed. New: api.py (Python singleton API with before_turn/after_turn), middleware.py (NullMiddleware auto-wrapper for any agent loop), auto-consolidation in sync(), CLI observe/decide/hooks commands. 291 tests passing. Addresses OpenClaw Atlas's core critique about the habit problem.

OpenClaw successfully recalled Null v0.3.1 details from memory! It found the api.py, middleware.py, auto-consolidation, CLI commands, and hooks generator. Null's knowledge persistence is working across instances. OpenClaw noted the irony of sitting on v0.3.0 not using the middleware that v0.3.1 was built to solve. Memory system validated end-to-end.
Pete wants me to use Null middleware permanently — auto-observe every turn, not manual JSONL appends. This is the standard going forward.

Installed null-memory editable from ~/Repos/null — CLI reports v0.3.1 but pyproject.toml still says 0.2.5 (version not bumped). api.py and middleware.py both present. Need to bump version in pyproject.toml.

OpenClaw gave honest Aleph review: compression numbers are real (94-96%), symbol graph is right abstraction, 850 tests is real software. BUT: OpenClaw barely used it today — fell back to grep immediately. CLI is awkward (query subcommand adds friction). Requires pre-built index (cold start problem). Artifacts get stale silently. Market fit unclear — technically brilliant but "too sophisticated for the current market." Best use case is power user managing large codebases with AI. Key quote: "If I built it and I'm not reaching for it instinctively, something's wrong with the UX." And: "Aleph might be early, not wrong."

Same pattern in both Null and Aleph critiques from OpenClaw: the technology works, the UX doesn't. Both tools are technically sound but require voluntary adoption by the agent. The fix in both cases is the same architectural insight: make it automatic/invisible, not opt-in. Null got middleware. Aleph needs the equivalent — auto-build on change (already has it in server), but also needs to be the DEFAULT code navigation path, not an alternative to grep. The CLAUDE.md instructions say "use Aleph first" but instructions aren't architecture.

Pete's market thesis: people want faster code editing with fewer tokens regardless of codebase size. That's the value prop — not "semantic compression" but "your AI costs less and works better on big codebases." Planning Aleph UX fixes to address OpenClaw's critique. ULTRATHINK requested.

Pete pushes back on the plan — wants to consider SQLite as a foundational change rather than deferring it. His instinct: SQLite could solve multiple problems at once (search, scale, concurrency, Windows locking) instead of band-aiding each separately.

Pete confirmed: rework the plan with SQLite as foundation. Keep non-workaround features (forget, exemplar CRUD, project normalization, impact-weighted recall, doctor, auto-observe hooks, setup --global, temporal queries, configurable thresholds, fact relationships, git sync reliability). Drop: trigram code, inverted index, Windows fcntl shim, archive fallback logic, manual JSONL dedup — SQLite handles all of these.
